subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Look around you right now. The air you are breathing, the soil outside your door, the skin on the back of your hand, and even the inside of your own stomach are all crowded with living things that are far too small for your eyes to see. We call these tiny living things microorganisms, which simply means 'very small organisms'. In this unit you will discover that although a single microorganism is smaller than a speck of dust, together they are among the most powerful and important living things on Earth.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Lower Secondary Science 0893 — Stage 7 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, working at your own pace with help from your AI tutor and your teacher, you will be able to:
Explain what a microorganism is and why a microscope is needed to see one.
Name and describe the main kinds of microorganism: bacteria, fungi (including yeast and mould), protozoa and algae, and explain why viruses are treated differently.
Describe how a single microorganism grows into a colony that is large enough to see.
Distinguish between microorganisms that are useful to people and those that are harmful.
Construct and interpret food chains and food webs, using arrows to show the direction of energy transfer.
Classify organisms as producers, consumers, herbivores, carnivores, predators, prey or decomposers.
Explain how microorganisms cause decay, and describe the important role of decomposers in returning nutrients to the soil.
Investigate how temperature and moisture affect the rate of decay, and draw a conclusion from your results.
Discuss the role of microorganisms in food, in health and in the wider environment, including in your own community.

concepts.title: What Is a Microorganism?
concepts.explanation: A microorganism is a living organism that is so small you cannot see it clearly without a microscope. The word is built from two parts: 'micro', meaning very small, and 'organism', meaning a living thing. Like every other living thing - like you, like a camel, like an acacia tree - microorganisms are made of cells. The difference is size. Many microorganisms are made of just a single cell, and that one cell is so tiny that thousands of them together would still be smaller than a grain of sand. It helps to picture…
concepts.example: There are several different kinds of microorganism. The main groups you will study are bacteria, microscopic fungi, protozoa and algae. Viruses are also extremely small, but scientists usually treat them separately, and…

concepts.title: Growing Microorganisms into Colonies
concepts.explanation: A single microorganism is far too small to see. So how do scientists study them? The trick is to let them grow. When a single bacterium or a single fungus cell is given warmth, moisture and food, it divides in two. Then each of those divides again, and again, and again. In only a day or two, one invisible cell becomes a colony of millions of cells - and a colony is big enough to see with your naked eye, as a small spot or patch. In a laboratory, scientists grow colonies on a special jelly called agar jelly, poured…
concepts.example: You can even tell the two main groups apart by looking. Colonies of bacteria usually have smooth, shiny edges, while colonies of fungi are usually fuzzy or furry with rough edges, because the fungus grows as fine…

concepts.title: Useful and Harmful Microorganisms
concepts.explanation: Because a few microorganisms cause serious disease, people often fear all of them. But the truth is more interesting and more balanced: microorganisms are among the most useful living things we have, and human life would be impossible without them. Think of your own kitchen. Bacteria turn fresh milk into yoghurt and into the sour fermented camel milk that many families drink. They do this by changing the sugar in the milk into acid, which is what gives yoghurt its sharp, pleasant taste and its thick texture.…
concepts.example: Some microorganisms, though, are harmful. When bacteria or mould grow on food we meant to eat, they spoil it and can make us ill if we eat it. This is why food left out in the heat goes bad so quickly, and why we must…

concepts.title: Food Chains - How Energy Flows
concepts.explanation: To understand where microorganisms fit into the natural world, you first need to understand how energy flows through all living things. Every living thing needs energy - to move, to grow, to stay warm, to reproduce. When Hassan eats a plate of bariis (rice) with goat meat, that food gives him energy for the day. But where did that energy come from in the first place? The answer is the Sun. Almost all the energy used by living things on Earth begins as sunlight. Green plants - and algae - can capture the energy in…
concepts.example: When an animal eats the plant, it takes in that stored food and so takes in the energy too. This passing of energy from one living thing to the next is called energy transfer. Animals cannot make their own food, so they…

concepts.title: Microorganisms and Decay
concepts.explanation: Now we can bring microorganisms into the story of energy and food. Think of a mango that has been left on the table for a week. It becomes soft, then brown, then covered in fuzzy patches. Each patch is a colony of millions of cells of microscopic fungi - this is mould, and we say the mango has become mouldy. The microorganisms have changed the mango; they have made it decay, or rot. Organisms that make things decay are called decomposers. The most important decomposers are certain bacteria and fungi. A decomposer…
concepts.example: At first, decay seems like nothing but a nuisance. No one wants to eat a rotten mango, and food spoiling in the heat is a real problem in a warm climate. But decay is one of the most important processes on the whole…

concepts.title: Placing Decomposers into Food Webs
concepts.explanation: You have learned that food webs show how energy flows from producers to consumers. But those webs left something out: what happens to the energy and nutrients when a plant or animal dies? This is where decomposers complete the picture. Decomposers feed on almost every organism after it dies, and on the waste that living animals leave behind. When they do this, energy from the dead organism is transferred into the decomposer - just as energy was transferred when a goat ate grass. So decomposers are a special kind…
concepts.example: To add decomposers to a food web properly, you would need to draw an arrow from every single plant and animal in the web to the decomposers, because eventually every organism dies and is broken down. That would make the…

explorations.prompt: On which day did you first see any growth? Describe it.
explorations.answer: On which day did you first see any growth? Describe it. The spots grew larger each day. Explain why, using the word 'colony'. Are the colonies smooth-edged or fuzzy? What does this tell you about whether they are bacteria or fungi? Where did the microorganisms come from, if the bread looked clean…

methods.title: Growing Mould on Bread (Colonies You Can See)
methods.example: Growing Mould on Bread (Colonies You Can See)
steps: Lightly dampen both slices of bread with a few drops of water. Do not soak them.
Leave both slices uncovered on a clean plate for about 30 minutes so microorganisms from the air can settle on them.
Place each slice in its own plastic bag or container and seal it firmly. Once sealed, do NOT open it again.
Label each bag with your name and the date.
Put both bags in a warm place away from direct sunlight, such as a kitchen shelf.

methods.title: Making Yoghurt with Helpful Bacteria
steps: Ask an adult to help you wash the container with boiling water, then let it cool. This kills unwanted microorganisms.
Warm the milk until it is pleasantly warm to the touch - not hot. Ask an adult to help.
Pour the warm milk into the clean container.
Stir in the tablespoon of yoghurt. This adds the living bacteria that will turn the milk into yoghurt.
Cover the container and wrap it in a blanket to keep it warm. Leave it undisturbed for 6 to 10 hours (overnight works well).

methods.title: Does Temperature Affect Decay?
steps: Dampen both slices with the SAME small amount of water so this variable is kept the same.
Seal each slice in its own bag and label them 'Warm' and 'Cool' with the date.
Put the 'Warm' bag in a warm place and the 'Cool' bag in the refrigerator or coolest place you have.
Keep both bags SEALED for the whole experiment.
Each day for 5 to 7 days, look at both slices through the bags and record how much mould has grown.

methods.title: Does Moisture Affect Decay? (Plan Your Own)
methods.example: Does Moisture Affect Decay? (Plan Your Own)
steps: Observe carefully and record what you see.
Compare your result with your hypothesis.

workedExamples.title: Worked example
workedExamples.prompt: Some questions in this unit ask you to organise results and calculate a mean (an average). Work through these carefully - every step is shown.
workedExamples.solution: Worked Example 1: Building a Food Chain Question: A chicken eats grain from a maize plant. Amina eats the chicken. Draw a food chain and label each organism. Step 1 - Find the producer. The maize plant makes its own food using sunlight, so it is the producer. It goes first. Step 2 - Follow who eats whom. The chicken eats the maize grain. Amina eats the chicken.

workedExamples.title: 1: Building a Food Chain
workedExamples.prompt: Question: A chicken eats grain from a maize plant. Amina eats the chicken. Draw a food chain and label each organism.
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Find the producer. The maize plant makes its own food using sunlight, so it is the producer. It goes first. Step 2 - Follow who eats whom. The chicken eats the maize grain. Amina eats the chicken. Step 3 - Draw the chain with arrows pointing in the direction energy flows: maize plant -> chicken -> Amina

workedExamples.title: 2: Calculating a Mean
workedExamples.prompt: Question: Khadija measures the length of five grass leaves growing next to cow dung: 11 cm, 13 cm, 9 cm, 12 cm and 8 cm. What is the mean (average) length?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Add up all the values. 11 + 13 + 9 + 12 + 8 = 53 cm. Step 2 - Count how many values there are. There are 5 leaves. Step 3 - Divide the total by the number of values. 53 divided by 5 = 10.6 cm. Answer: The mean length of the grass leaves next to the cow dung is 10.6 cm. To compare, if the five leaves far from dung measured 9, 10, 6, 7 and 9 cm, their total is 41 cm and their mean is 41 divided by 5 = 8.2 cm. The grass near the dung is taller on average, which fits the idea that decomposers broke the dung…

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: Why are viruses usually NOT called true microorganisms? (a) they are too large; (b) they are not made of cells and cannot reproduce on their own; (c) they are always harmless; (d) they only live in soil.
workedExamples.solution: 4 (b) A virus is not a cell and can only reproduce inside a living cell, so it is treated separately.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: A group of cells produced when one microorganism divides again and again is called a: (a) colony; (b) tissue; (c) nutrient; (d) spore.
workedExamples.solution: 5 (a) A colony forms when one microorganism divides again and again into millions of cells.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: In the food chain grass -> goat -> lion, the grass is the: (a) consumer; (b) predator; (c) producer; (d) decomposer.
workedExamples.solution: 6 (c) Grass makes its own food from sunlight, so it is the producer.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: An animal that eats only plants is called a: (a) carnivore; (b) herbivore; (c) producer; (d) decomposer.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: The arrows in a food chain show: (a) which animal is biggest; (b) the direction energy flows; (c) where animals live; (d) the order of size.
workedExamples.solution: 8 (b) Arrows show the direction energy flows, from food to eater.

practice.prompt: A microorganism is best described as: (a) any small animal; (b) a living thing too small to see without a microscope; (c) a type of insect; (d) a non-living particle.
practice.answer: 1 (b) A microorganism is a living thing too small to see clearly without a microscope.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a single-celled fungus? (a) a mushroom; (b) a bacterium; (c) yeast; (d) a virus.
practice.answer: 2 (c) Yeast is a single-celled fungus. A mushroom is a large fungus, not single-celled.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Bacteria differ from plant and animal cells because they: (a) are much larger; (b) have no cell wall; (c) have no nucleus; (d) contain chlorophyll.
practice.answer: 3 (c) Bacteria have a cell wall but no nucleus, unlike plant and animal cells.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Why are viruses usually NOT called true microorganisms? (a) they are too large; (b) they are not made of cells and cannot reproduce on their own; (c) they are always harmless; (d) they only live in soil.
practice.answer: 4 (b) A virus is not a cell and can only reproduce inside a living cell, so it is treated separately.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A group of cells produced when one microorganism divides again and again is called a: (a) colony; (b) tissue; (c) nutrient; (d) spore.
practice.answer: 5 (a) A colony forms when one microorganism divides again and again into millions of cells.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In the food chain grass -> goat -> lion, the grass is the: (a) consumer; (b) predator; (c) producer; (d) decomposer.
practice.answer: 6 (c) Grass makes its own food from sunlight, so it is the producer.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: An animal that eats only plants is called a: (a) carnivore; (b) herbivore; (c) producer; (d) decomposer.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The arrows in a food chain show: (a) which animal is biggest; (b) the direction energy flows; (c) where animals live; (d) the order of size.
practice.answer: 8 (b) Arrows show the direction energy flows, from food to eater.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Organisms that break down dead material and cause decay are called: (a) producers; (b) predators; (c) decomposers; (d) herbivores.
practice.answer: 9 (c) Decomposers break down dead material and cause decay.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which two groups are the most important decomposers? (a) fish and birds; (b) bacteria and fungi; (c) insects and worms; (d) algae and protozoa.
practice.answer: 10 (b) Bacteria and fungi are the most important decomposers.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Any substance made by a living thing, such as fruit or wood, is called: (a) a nutrient; (b) organic matter; (c) a mineral; (d) a colony.
practice.answer: 11 (b) Organic matter is any substance made by a living thing.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Decay usually happens fastest when conditions are: (a) cold and dry; (b) warm and moist; (c) cold and moist; (d) warm and dry.
practice.answer: 12 (b) Warm, moist conditions let decomposers grow and feed fastest.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Growing Mould on Bread (Colonies You Can See)
activities.materials: 2 slices of bread (any kind); A little clean water and a spoon; 2 clear, sealable plastic bags or 2 small clear containers with lids; A marker pen and sticky tape for labelling
steps: Lightly dampen both slices of bread with a few drops of water. Do not soak them.
Leave both slices uncovered on a clean plate for about 30 minutes so microorganisms from the air can settle on them.
Place each slice in its own plastic bag or container and seal it firmly. Once sealed, do NOT open it again.
Label each bag with your name and the date.
Put both bags in a warm place away from direct sunlight, such as a kitchen shelf.

activities.title: Making Yoghurt with Helpful Bacteria
activities.materials: 1 cup of fresh milk (cow, goat or camel); 1 tablespoon of plain shop-bought or homemade yoghurt (this contains the living bacteria); A clean container with a lid or a clean cloth cover; A clean spoon; a warm blanket or towel to wrap the container
steps: Ask an adult to help you wash the container with boiling water, then let it cool. This kills unwanted microorganisms.
Warm the milk until it is pleasantly warm to the touch - not hot. Ask an adult to help.
Pour the warm milk into the clean container.
Stir in the tablespoon of yoghurt. This adds the living bacteria that will turn the milk into yoghurt.
Cover the container and wrap it in a blanket to keep it warm. Leave it undisturbed for 6 to 10 hours (overnight works well).

activities.title: Does Temperature Affect Decay?
activities.materials: 2 similar slices of bread; A little water; 2 sealable plastic bags or clear containers with lids; A marker pen for labelling; Access to a warm place and a cool place (such as a refrigerator or cool store)
steps: Dampen both slices with the SAME small amount of water so this variable is kept the same.
Seal each slice in its own bag and label them 'Warm' and 'Cool' with the date.
Put the 'Warm' bag in a warm place and the 'Cool' bag in the refrigerator or coolest place you have.
Keep both bags SEALED for the whole experiment.
Each day for 5 to 7 days, look at both slices through the bags and record how much mould has grown.

activities.title: Does Moisture Affect Decay? (Plan Your Own)
steps: Follow the investigation plan in your experiments book.

activities.title: Observe: Microorganisms and Decay
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of microorganisms and decay around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: Placing Decomposers into Food Webs
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of placing decomposers into food webs around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: too that not every decomposer is a microorganism. The toadstools growing on a fallen log are the visible part of a fungus, but the hidden threads inside the log are breaking the wood down - so that large fungus is a decomposer even though you can see it easily. And if a slug eats the toadstool, the energy the slug gains can be traced all the way back, through the fungus and the dead wood, to the tree that first captured it from the Sun.

terms: A living thing too small to see clearly without a microscope.
Single-celled microbes with a cell wall but no nucleus; smaller than plant/animal cells.
Cell-walled organisms with a nucleus that never make their own food; include yeast, mould, mushrooms.
A single-celled fungus that feeds on sugar and makes dough rise.
A visible fungus that grows as a fuzzy covering on food and other organic matter.
A tiny non-cell particle that only reproduces inside a living cell.
Single-celled microbes with animal-like cells; one kind causes malaria.
Single-celled microbes with plant-like cells that make food from sunlight.
Millions of cells grown from one microorganism dividing repeatedly; large enough to see.
A nutrient jelly used to grow microorganisms in a Petri dish.
Completely free of all living things.

vocabulary.meaning: A living thing too small to see clearly without a microscope.
vocabulary.example: In this unit you will discover that although a single microorganism is smaller than a speck of dust, together they are among the most powerful and important living things on Earth.
Single-celled microorganisms with a cell wall but no nucleus; smaller than plant or animal cells.

vocabulary.meaning: Single-celled microbes with a cell wall but no nucleus; smaller than plant/animal cells.
vocabulary.example: In this unit you will meet the main kinds of microorganism - bacteria, fungi, viruses, protozoa and algae.

vocabulary.meaning: Cell-walled organisms with a nucleus that never make their own food; include yeast, mould, mushrooms.
vocabulary.example: In this unit you will meet the main kinds of microorganism - bacteria, fungi, viruses, protozoa and algae.

vocabulary.meaning: A single-celled fungus that feeds on sugar and makes dough rise.
vocabulary.example: The dusty, powdery bloom you sometimes see on the skin of grapes or dates is made of millions of yeast cells feeding on the sugar in the fruit.

vocabulary.meaning: A visible fungus that grows as a fuzzy covering on food and other organic matter.
vocabulary.example: Mould, the fuzzy growth on old bread or a forgotten mango, is another fungus; it grows as fine threads and is a very common decomposer.

vocabulary.meaning: A tiny non-cell particle that only reproduces inside a living cell.
vocabulary.example: Because it is not a cell and cannot feed, grow or reproduce on its own, most scientists do not call a virus a true microorganism, and many do not even call it fully 'alive'.

vocabulary.meaning: Single-celled microbes with animal-like cells; one kind causes malaria.
vocabulary.example: In this unit you will meet the main kinds of microorganism - bacteria, fungi, viruses, protozoa and algae.

vocabulary.meaning: Single-celled microbes with plant-like cells that make food from sunlight.
vocabulary.example: In this unit you will meet the main kinds of microorganism - bacteria, fungi, viruses, protozoa and algae.

vocabulary.meaning: Millions of cells grown from one microorganism dividing repeatedly; large enough to see.
vocabulary.example: Describe how a single microorganism grows into a colony that is large enough to see.

vocabulary.meaning: A nutrient jelly used to grow microorganisms in a Petri dish.
vocabulary.example: In a laboratory, scientists grow colonies on a special jelly called agar jelly, poured into a shallow round dish with a lid called a Petri dish.

vocabulary.meaning: Completely free of all living things.
vocabulary.example: Before use, both the dish and the jelly must be sterile, which means every living thing on them has already been killed.

commonMistakes: Calling a virus a small bacterium.
A virus is not a cell.

connections.text: Cells (Year 7 earlier units): microorganisms are made of cells, just like plants and animals.
Chemical reactions (next unit): decay and milk souring are slow chemical changes.
clean water, hand-washing and cooking food link directly to controlling microbes.
composting, soil fertility and food preservation in a hot climate.
cleanliness as part of faith, and caring for the earth as a steward (khalifah).
Find out how cheese and other fermented foods are made using microorganisms.

fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 6 (c) Grass makes its own food from sunlight, so it is the producer.

fluency.prompt: An animal that eats only plants is called a: (a) carnivore; (b) herbivore; (c) producer; (d) decomposer.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

fluency.prompt: The arrows in a food chain show: (a) which animal is biggest; (b) the direction energy flows; (c) where animals live; (d) the order of size.
fluency.answer: 8 (b) Arrows show the direction energy flows, from food to eater.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 8 (b) Arrows show the direction energy flows, from food to eater.

fluency.prompt: Organisms that break down dead material and cause decay are called: (a) producers; (b) predators; (c) decomposers; (d) herbivores.
fluency.answer: 9 (c) Decomposers break down dead material and cause decay.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 9 (c) Decomposers break down dead material and cause decay.

fluency.prompt: Which two groups are the most important decomposers? (a) fish and birds; (b) bacteria and fungi; (c) insects and worms; (d) algae and protozoa.
fluency.answer: 10 (b) Bacteria and fungi are the most important decomposers.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 10 (b) Bacteria and fungi are the most important decomposers.

fluency.prompt: Any substance made by a living thing, such as fruit or wood, is called: (a) a nutrient; (b) organic matter; (c) a mineral; (d) a colony.
fluency.answer: 11 (b) Organic matter is any substance made by a living thing.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 11 (b) Organic matter is any substance made by a living thing.

fluency.prompt: Decay usually happens fastest when conditions are: (a) cold and dry; (b) warm and moist; (c) cold and moist; (d) warm and dry.
fluency.answer: 12 (b) Warm, moist conditions let decomposers grow and feed fastest.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 12 (b) Warm, moist conditions let decomposers grow and feed fastest.

realProblems.prompt: Amina's family leaves a pot of cooked rice out overnight in the warm kitchen. By morning it smells sour. Explain what has happened and suggest two ways to prevent it.
realProblems.answer: Microorganisms (bacteria) grew in the warm rice overnight and made it decay, producing the sour smell. Prevent it by keeping the rice cool (refrigerator/cool store) or covered, or by reheating thoroughly and not leaving it out in the heat.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Microorganisms (bacteria) grew in the warm rice overnight and made it decay, producing the sour smell. Prevent it by keeping the rice cool (refrigerator/cool store) or covered, or by reheating thoroughly and not leaving it out in the heat.

realProblems.prompt: A farmer near the Jubba River sees that grass grows greener next to cattle dung. Explain how decomposers and dung help the grass grow.
realProblems.answer: Decomposer fungi and bacteria break down the dung and return its nutrients to the soil. The grass roots take up these nutrients, so the grass grows greener and taller near the dung.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Decomposer fungi and bacteria break down the dung and return its nutrients to the soil. The grass roots take up these nutrients, so the grass grows greener and taller near the dung.

realProblems.prompt: Halima makes yoghurt but it sets very slowly. Suggest one change she could make to speed it up, and explain why it would work.
realProblems.answer: Keep the milk warmer (but not hot). Bacteria are living things that grow and work faster in warmth, so the milk turns to yoghurt more quickly. (Also acceptable: add a little more yoghurt starter to add more bacteria.)
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Keep the milk warmer (but not hot). Bacteria are living things that grow and work faster in warmth, so the milk turns to yoghurt more quickly. (Also acceptable: add a little more yoghurt starter to add more bacteria.)

realProblems.prompt: Explain why sleeping under a bed net helps protect a child from malaria, referring to the microorganism and how it spreads.
realProblems.answer: Malaria is caused by a protozoan spread by mosquito bites. A bed net stops mosquitoes from biting the sleeping child, so the protozoan cannot be passed on, reducing the risk of malaria.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Malaria is caused by a protozoan spread by mosquito bites. A bed net stops mosquitoes from biting the sleeping child, so the protozoan cannot be passed on, reducing the risk of malaria.

realProblems.prompt: A family starts a compost pit for fruit peelings and vegetable scraps. Explain what happens in the pit and why the finished compost is good for a garden.
realProblems.answer: Decomposers break down the peelings and scraps, turning them into rich compost full of nutrients. Added to garden soil, these nutrients help plants grow well. It also recycles waste instead of throwing it away.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Decomposers break down the peelings and scraps, turning them into rich compost full of nutrients. Added to garden soil, these nutrients help plants grow well. It also recycles waste instead of throwing it away.

realProblems.prompt: Explain, using the science of microorganisms, why washing hands with soap before eating helps keep a family healthy.
realProblems.answer: Hands can carry harmful microorganisms picked up from surfaces or the toilet. Soap and water wash these microbes away, so they are not transferred to food and into the body, keeping the family healthy.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Hands can carry harmful microorganisms picked up from surfaces or the toilet. Soap and water wash these microbes away, so they are not transferred to food and into the body, keeping the family healthy.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain what a microorganism is and why we need a microscope to study one.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A microorganism is a living thing so small it can only be seen clearly with a microscope. A single one is far smaller than one of our own cells, so our eyes alone cannot see it - only a microscope makes it visible.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the four main kinds of microorganism and give one example of each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Bacteria (e.g. the bacteria in yoghurt); fungi (e.g. yeast or mould); protozoa (e.g. the malaria protozoan); algae (e.g. green algae in pond water).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain how one invisible microorganism can grow into a colony you can see.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: One microorganism divides into two, those two each divide, and so on. In a day or two, one invisible cell becomes millions of cells - a colony - which is large enough to see.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Give two examples of useful microorganisms and say what each one does.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Yeast makes dough rise for bread and canjeero; bacteria turn milk into yoghurt and fermented camel milk. (Also acceptable: gut bacteria aid digestion; soil decomposers recycle nutrients.)

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why must a Petri dish and agar jelly be sterile before use?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: So that no unwanted microorganisms are already growing on them. If they were not sterile, other microbes would grow and spoil the experiment.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain why decomposers are important for keeping plants growing.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Producer: makes its own food from sunlight. Consumer: gets energy by eating other organisms. Herbivore: eats only plants. Carnivore: eats only other animals.

questions.question: Which of these is a single-celled fungus?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Yeast is a single-celled fungus. A mushroom is a large fungus, not single-celled.

questions.question: Bacteria differ from plant and animal cells because they:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Bacteria have a cell wall but no nucleus, unlike plant and animal cells.

questions.question: Why are viruses usually NOT called true microorganisms?
options: they are not made of cells and cannot reproduce on their own;
questions.answer: they are not made of cells and cannot reproduce on their own;
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A virus is not a cell and can only reproduce inside a living cell, so it is treated separately.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, working at your own pace with help from your AI tutor and your teacher, you will be able to:
I can explain what a microorganism is and why a microscope is needed to see one.
I can name and describe the main kinds of microorganism: bacteria, fungi (including yeast and mould), protozoa and algae, and explain why viruses are treated differently.
I can describe how a single microorganism grows into a colony that is large enough to see.
I can distinguish between microorganisms that are useful to people and those that are harmful.
I can construct and interpret food chains and food webs, using arrows to show the direction of energy transfer.
I can classify organisms as producers, consumers, herbivores, carnivores, predators, prey or decomposers.
I can explain how microorganisms cause decay, and describe the important role of decomposers in returning nutrients to the soil.
